response:
Sarah owns a small retail shop that supports local factory workers by selling their handmade products. She decides to run a promotional week where she offers a 15% discount on all items produced by the factory workers. If a customer purchases a handcrafted table originally priced at $200 and a set of factory-made chairs originally priced at $150, how much does the customer pay for these items during the promotional week? Remember to apply the discount to each item separately. 

*** 

During the promotional week, the customer will pay 15% less for each item. The handcrafted table originally priced at $200 will cost $200 - (15% of $200) = $200 - $30 = $170. The factory-made chairs originally priced at $150 will cost $150 - (15% of $150) = $150 - $22.50 = $127.50. The total amount the customer will pay for both items is $170 + $127.50 = $297.50.

Reach for the final amount, the customer will pay $297.50 for the handcrafted table and factory-made chairs during the promotional week. The discount is applied to each item separately, ensuring that the customer pays the reduced price for each product. The customer will receive a total of $297.50 for the two items. 

*** 

The promotional week offers a 15% discount on all items, which means the customer will pay less for each item. The handcrafted table originally priced at $200 will cost $170 after the discount, and the factory-made chairs originally priced at $150 will cost $127.50 after the discount. The total amount the customer will pay is $170 + $127.50 = $297.50. 

The customer will pay $297.50 for the two items during the promotional week. The discount is applied to each item separately, ensuring that the customer pays the reduced price for each product. The customer will receive a total of $297.50 for the two items.
